title: Your First Salary & a Simple Budget
summary: Your take-home pay is smaller than your CTC. A simple way to handle it is to split each month between needs, wants, and savings — and to watch out for lifestyle inflation as your income grows.

sections.heading: CTC vs take-home
sections.body: Your offer letter shows CTC (cost to company), but you actually receive take-home pay after taxes, PF and deductions. Always budget on take-home, not CTC.

sections.heading: A starter split
sections.body: A common rule of thumb is roughly 50% needs (rent, food, transport), 30% wants, 20% savings & investments. Adjust to your city and situation — the point is to decide on purpose, not by accident.

sections.heading: Needs vs wants
sections.body: Needs are things you can't easily skip; wants make life nicer but are optional. Most 'budget leaks' come from wants quietly turning into fixed monthly habits.

quiz.prompt: You should plan your monthly budget around:
In a 50/30/20 style split, the 20% is for:
In the 50/30/20 starter split, roughly what share goes to needs like rent, food and transport?
Why is take-home pay usually smaller than your CTC?
options: Because of taxes, PF and other deductions
Because banks keep a portion of every salary

quiz.prompt: According to the lesson, most 'budget leaks' come from:
options: Wants quietly turning into fixed monthly habits

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
